A Hands-On Guide to Adding Google reCAPTCHA v3 to Your Project
A hands-on guide to adding reCAPTCHA to your project, including why to use it and what the pros and cons are.
reCAPTCHA is a verification service provided by Google.
What? You don't know what reCAPTCHA is? No worries, look at this animation and you'll recognize it:
I'm sure you've seen this online — the "I'm not a robot" service.
reCAPTCHA mainly prevents bots from doing malicious things — fake signups, fake logins, spam comments, etc.
I'll walk through how to add reCAPTCHA to your project. This post uses the newer reCAPTCHA v3 service.
and click "Get Started With Enterprise" at the top.
On the welcome page, just change the project name and check the service items (if you've used Cloud Platform before the UI might look a bit different, but it's basically the same).
After waiting, you'll land on the Google Cloud page. reCAPTCHA service is now registered.
You'll be redirected to this page:

Platform type: pick the platform your project runs on — website, Android, or iOS (we'll pick website)

In short: when the frontend submits the form, it first sends the user's behavior to Google for analysis. Google returns a token, which we send to the backend along with
. The backend uses both to determine if it's a real user and to score them.
If you ran with Google's template directly, you'd hit this error:
Error: Could not load the default credentials. Browse to https://cloud.google.com/docs/authentication/getting-started for more information.
looks for default credentials by default.
We need to create a service account in Google Cloud Platform. It generates a JSON file — that's our credential.
, find your project, click "IAM & Admin" on the left, then "Service Accounts".
Click "Create Service Account" and fill out the form. You can follow mine:
For the second step "Grant this service account access to project (optional)", recommend setting it to "Editor":
Skip the third step and click "Done". The service account is created. Click on it.
Click "Keys", find "Add Key", then "Create new key".
A modal pops up — choose JSON and click "Create".
You'll get a JSON file — that's the credential.
Open the JSON. It looks like:

to build credentials. Update the code:
Now click "Submit" on the frontend, send the request to the backend, and the backend works correctly with a returned score.
The score is between 0 and 1. 0 means highly likely a bot. 1 means highly likely a real user. When Google flags it as a bot, you can trigger reCAPTCHA verification to block malicious actions.
A little more on the score Google returns:

Google calls this the "risk score". It's based on mouse movements, click patterns, browser environment, and more. Range is 0.0 - 1.0. Closer to 1.0 = more likely real user; closer to 0.0 = more likely bot.
What do you do with the score? Set thresholds — e.g. 0.5, 0.7, or 0.9. When the score crosses a threshold, trigger different verification flows to limit bot abuse.
"Wait — what about Google's 'I'm not a robot' checkbox? Doesn't that always show up?"
Since reCAPTCHA went from v2 to v3, Google moved from those disruptive image puzzles to score-based detection. Users no longer go through tedious verification — the score determines bot likelihood.
and below: trigger SMS, email verification, etc.
and above: let them in directly, or use a simpler verification
Can you mix reCAPTCHA v2 and v3?
Sure. Use your threshold to decide when to trigger v2 verification. Makes your site more secure.
But that's a topic for another post. If interested, I can cover mixing v2 and v3 next time.
